Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss Complaint
Off Calendar Jul-31-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. Donald Sherrill, Esq. of Hunt & Henriques, LLP (tel: 800-680-24260 for the Plaintiff.
Defendant Ebru Dogan, in pro per present.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT EBRU DOGAN's Motion To Dismiss Complaint.

Matter argued and submitted. Upon conclusion, the Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The motion to dismiss is denied.

Defendant Ebru Dogan did not file a proof of service of her motion to dismiss. Capital One acknowledges receiving the motion, albeit late, but has waived this defect. The requirement for serving an opposing party with sufficient notice of a motion is set out in Code of Civil Procedure 1005(b) and related statutes, and is sixteen court days in advance of the motion, with two court days added where electronic service is used. Dogan is reminded to follow the statutory requirements for future filings.

On the merits, court treats the demurrer (which comes after Dogan has answered the complaint) as a nonstatutory motion for judgment on the pleadings.

Dogan first contends that the complaint fails to state a claim. The court disagrees; the elements of breach of a credit card contract are adequately alleged.

Dogan next contends that the complaint should be dismissed because Capital One has not filed a signed contract, validation of the debt, or an accurate accounting of the amount owed. On a motion to dismiss, the court must assume the facts set out in the complaint as true. Thus, the truth of Capital One's allegations is accepted for purposes of a motion to dismiss. Dogan provides no authority that Capital One must satisfy FDCPA procedural requirements in order to plead a breach of contract claim.

Dogan next contends that the action is time-barred. Capital One pleads a breach on or about February 14, 2024, a date within the four-year limitations period for a breach of contract action. This fact must be accepted as true for purposes of a motion to dismiss.

Finally, Dogan contends that the court lacks jurisdiction over her and over the controversy. By filing an answer, Dogan has waived any jurisdictional defects. Construing her motion as a forum non conveniens motion, Dogan does not provide evidence or argument that there is another forum where all of the claims can be litigated more conveniently.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling, with a cover letter demonstrating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)
